item.title: Graham Platner is forcing Dems to change
item.description: Are voters rejecting career politicians? The 2026 midterms are already being shaken up by populist firebrands and antiestablishment outsiders. In this episode of "Make It Make Sense," Kate Andrews, Carine Hajjar and James Desio break down Graham Platner’s controversial momentum in Maine, James Talarico's polished strategy in Texas and Spencer Pratt’s AI-fueled mayoral run in Los Angeles. Are voters ready to overhaul the political elite, or are these candidates just the latest shiny objects in the national culture war?

item.title: Trump’s redistricting war forces a surrender in South Carolina
item.description: Cynical redistricting map dilutes Black voting power to target a kingmaker.

item.title: Notes on new parenthood
item.description: Edith Pritchett cartoon on challenges of becoming a parent and raising a baby.

item.title: The problem with celebrity politicians
item.description:
So many influencers, celebrities, billionaires and media personalities seem convinced they can follow President Donald Trump’s path from fame to political power. But are they learning the right lessons from his rise?
Host Megan McArdle explores the rise of the celebrity candidate, from Ronald Reagan to Arnold Schwarzenegger to Trump himself. She explains how parasocial relationships shape modern elections, and why Trump’s success depended on a unique mix of media savviness, outsider status, timing and luck that may be impossible to replicate. And she argues that if America wants a more stable political future, it will need to return to a more normal kind of politics.

item.title: Fingers crossed
item.description: Michael Ramirez cartoon on Donald Trump, Xi Jinping summit in China.

item.title: I have never felt that the U.S. government was imposing beliefs on me until now
item.description: Readers on the White House's prayer festival and solutions to gun violence.

item.title: Why won’t Democrats moderate on immigration? Here’s a clue.
item.description: Open borders are existential for the party.

item.title: A rift between Pope Leo and the White House deepens
item.description: The Vatican and the Constitution show the way on the Iran war.

item.title: The return of the moderate Spanberger
item.description: After the gerrymandering debacle, Virginia’s governor is trying to reclaim her pragmatic image. She’s off to a good start.

item.title: A desperately needed reset at the FDA
item.description: What Marty Makary’s successor can learn from his mistakes.

item.title: Has the U.S. lost the Iran war?
item.description: This episode of "Make It Make Sense," moderated by James Hohmann, asks a critical geopolitical question on where the United States stands with Iran. Damir Marusic and Carine Hajjar debate whether the U.S. has achieved its strategic objectives and if pursuing a new diplomatic deal with Tehran is actually worthwhile. Also, can the Strait of Hormuz be opened without total war? And why is Gen Z against war in Iran?
